steps.title: Identify the behavioral shift electric lighting would cause
steps.content:
The researchers conclude that electric lighting in the late nineteenth century prompted a behavioral shift visible in how fiction references time.

The key prediction: if lighting enabled evening activity, novels published after its spread should contain significantly more references to late-night activities than earlier novels.

steps.title: Find the evidence matching this prediction
steps.content:
Answer D directly demonstrates the predicted shift: novels after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities after 10 p.m., confirming that electric lighting enabled the behavioral change toward evening activity.
  • Choice A — Novels published after the year 1800 include the clock phrase 10 a.m. less often than novels published before the year 1800 do.: Goes the wrong direction — a daytime decline does not demonstrate the predicted shift toward evening activity, and the timeframe (1800) predates the researchers' late-nineteenth-century mechanism. ✗
  • Choice B — Among novels published in the nineteenth century, implied time references become steadily more common than clock phrases as publication dates approach 1900.: Addresses reference format and style rather than the substantive shift toward late-night activity; says nothing about when in the day activities occur. ✗
  • Choice C — The time references of noon (12 p.m.) and midnight (12 a.m.) are used with roughly the same frequency in the novels.: Shows no shift over time and does not isolate the post-lighting period; equal frequency contradicts a behavioral shift toward evening. ✗
  • Choice D — Novels published after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities occurring after 10 p.m. than do novels from earlier periods.: Directly matches the predicted behavioral shift — more late-night activity references appear after the spread of electric lighting. ✓
D is correct.
